pull.title: test: enable -Wunused-function in test suite (Fix #1831)
pull.body:
This PR addresses issue #1831 by enabling the `-Wunused-function` compiler warning within the test suite. 

Currently, `-Wno-unused-function` is passed globally to disable warnings about unused functions, making it too easy to write a test case but forget to actually call it. To catch untested helper functions safely, this PR uses GCC/Clang pragmas scoped strictly to the body of the test files.

### Changes Made:
- Added `#pragma GCC diagnostic warning "-Wunused-function"` directly after the `#include` statements in `src/tests.c`, `src/tests_exhaustive.c`, `src/ctime_tests.c`, and `src/unit_test.c`. 
Fixes #1831

repo.name: secp256k1-kallal
repo.description: Optimized C library for EC operations on curve secp256k1

events.body:
Thanks for the PR. :)

Concept ACK

Thanks for the quick update.

ACK mod that this should be squashed

Could you squash the 2nd and 3rd commit into the 1st one and force-push?

We follow the same workflow as Bitcoin Core here, see https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#squashing-commits for background.

~From https://github.com/bitcoin-core/secp256k1/issues/1831#issue-4010176205:~
~> ... we should enable -Wunused-function at least in the test suite.~

~Could this be enabled as well?~

Concept ACK.

An alternative approach would be appending `-Wunused-function` to the compiler flags when building test targets. However, that would require changing and then maintaining for both build systems.

From https://github.com/bitcoin-core/secp256k1/issues/1831#issue-4010176205:
>  It's too easy to add a test but forget to call it.

Tested this scenario. Works as expected.

> @kallal79 
> 
> 
> 
> > Could you squash the 2nd and 3rd commit into the 1st one and force-push?
> 
> 
> 
> Just checking: Did you see this request? (I saw that you gave a few thumbs-ups in later comment.)


And https://github.com/bitcoin-core/secp256k1/pull/1865#discussion_r3389257862?

comments.body: A `push` without a `pop` doesn't makes a lot of sense. I think the cleanest thing is to add a `pop` at the end of each test file.

Also our macro `SECP256K1_GNUC_PREREQ(4, 2)` would be preferable over `defined(__GNUC__)`. 

(This is maybe questionable when it comes to these old compiler versions but as long as we have the macro, we should use it.)

While the `-Wno-unused-function` diagnostics has been available since GCC 3.0, the support for `#pragma GCC diagnostic` was [introduced](https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc-4.2.4/gcc/Diagnostic-Pragmas.html) only in GCC 4.2, and the `push`/`pop` variants in [GCC 4.6](https://gcc.gnu.org/gcc-4.6/changes.html). Since this PR uses `diagnostic push`, the guard should be `SECP256K1_GNUC_PREREQ(4, 6)`. Note that this check evaluates to false under Clang, which masquerades as GCC 4.2.x, so an additional `defined(__clang__)` check is required.

(Oh, I had marked this conversation resolved already, so I missed your comment @hebasto.)

You're right. I suggested the wrong version here. (I trusted ChatGPT 5.4 but apparently you can't even trust it for these simple things... -.-) I think we should really get rid of the `SECP256K1_GNUC_PREREQ` macro. 

@kallal79 
For now, I suggest to change this back to what you had proposed initially (`#if defined(__GNUC__)`) because this will work both for GCC and Clang. Sorry for the back and forth...
